title: Bourbon Spain
subtitle: 1700–1808 · War of Succession, Enlightenment and Reform

categories.label: Power
categories.subtitle: The Bourbon kings — dynastic rupture and enlightened reform

entries.label: Philip V — the War of Spanish Succession
entries.hint: Philip V, grandson of Louis XIV, inherits Spain from the last Habsburg — triggering the 13-year War of Spanish Succession that involves most of Europe; the Peace of Utrecht (1713) confirms him as king but strips Spain of the Netherlands, Sardinia, Naples and Gibraltar.
entries.note: Philip V's accession: Gaceta de Madrid, Nov 1700. Peace of Utrecht: AGS Estado; also in the British National Archives (SP 108/402). The loss of Gibraltar (Treaty of Utrecht Art.10, 1713): text in Davenport (1917). Philip V abdicated briefly 1724 (to his son Luis I who died after 7 months) — documented in Gaceta de Madrid. Kamen (2001), Philip V of Spain: the standard English biography.

entries.label: Charles III — Spain's great enlightened monarch
entries.hint: Charles III is the paradigm of Bourbon enlightened despotism — expels the Jesuits (1767), centralises administration, promotes trade, builds roads and canals, reforms the colonies; his reforms transform Spain but create tensions that will explode in 1808.
entries.note: Jesuit expulsion decree: Pragmática Sanción, 27 Feb 1767, Gaceta de Madrid. The Bourbon administrative reforms (Nueva Planta decrees): AGS. Lynch (1989), Bourbon Spain 1700–1808: the standard English study. Charles III's building programme in Madrid: documented in Archivo de la Villa de Madrid. Herr (1958), The Eighteenth-Century Revolution in Spain: analyses the reform programme.

entries.label: Charles IV and Godoy — the road to 1808
entries.hint: Charles IV's weak rule is dominated by his chief minister Manuel Godoy — their disastrous alliance with Napoleon leads to Trafalgar (1805), the Aranjuez uprising (March 1808), and Napoleon's replacement of the Bourbons with his brother Joseph.
entries.note: The Aranjuez uprising (Motín de Aranjuez) 17–19 Mar 1808: contemporary press and AGS. Godoy's appointment records: AGS Estado. The Treaty of Fontainebleau (27 Oct 1807) allowing French troops through Spain: AGS Estado legajo 8174. Charles IV's abdication 19 Mar 1808: Gaceta de Madrid 20 Mar 1808. La Parra (2002), Manuel Godoy: the standard modern biography.

categories.label: Conflict
categories.subtitle: War of Succession, colonial wars and the road to Trafalgar

entries.label: Battle of Almansa — Bourbon victory in Spain
entries.hint: The Duke of Berwick defeats the Austro-British allied army at Almansa — securing Castile and Aragon for Philip V; the decisive battle of the War of Spanish Succession in Spain, fought by a French marshal commanding a Spanish army against a British-led force.
entries.note: Military records: AGS Guerra Moderna. The battle's outcome: confirmed across French, British and Spanish sources. The Decreto de Nueva Planta for Valencia (29 Jun 1707): issued directly after Almansa — Gaceta de Madrid. The paradox: a French marshal won Castile for the Spanish crown. Kamen (1969), The War of Succession in Spain 1700–1715: the standard study.

entries.label: Battle of Trafalgar — end of Spanish naval power
entries.hint: Nelson defeats the combined Franco-Spanish fleet at Trafalgar — 18 Spanish ships lost, 2,500 Spanish dead; Spain's naval power is broken permanently, making it impossible to maintain the American empire; Napoleon's invasion follows three years later.
entries.note: The battle: documented in both British National Archives (ADM 1/404, Nelson's log) and AGS Marina (Spanish fleet records). Spanish casualties: AGS Marina. The Spanish flagship Santísima Trinidad (112 guns, largest warship in the world): its last log entry is in AGS. Adkins (2004), Trafalgar: uses both British and Spanish archives. Spain's fleet never recovered: documented in AGS Marina statistics.

entries.label: Motín de Esquilache — the riots that triggered the Jesuit expulsion
entries.hint: In March 1766 Madrid erupts in riots against Charles III's Italian minister Esquilache — ostensibly over a ban on long cloaks and broad-brimmed hats, but driven by bread price inflation; the king flees the capital; the Crown blames the Jesuits for instigating the riots, using this as the formal pretext for their expulsion in February 1767.
entries.note: The Motín de Esquilache March 1766: AGS and contemporary press. Charles III's flight to Aranjuez: documented. The secret investigation blaming Jesuits: AGS Gracia y Justicia. Lynch (1989), Bourbon Spain pp.254–261: the standard analysis of the Esquilache-expulsion connection. The Pragmática Sanción (1767) gives no stated reason — the Esquilache connection is documented only in the secret AGS report.

categories.label: Order
categories.subtitle: Bourbon centralisation, the Nueva Planta and colonial reform

entries.label: Decreto de Nueva Planta — abolishing Catalan and Aragonese law
entries.hint: Following the War of Succession, Philip V abolishes the traditional laws and institutions of Aragon, Valencia, Mallorca and Catalonia — replacing them with Castilian law; this is the origin of the Catalan grievance that has never been fully resolved.
entries.note: Decreto de Nueva Planta for Catalonia (16 Jan 1716): Gaceta de Madrid, 18 Jan 1716 — text published. The Valencia decree (29 Jun 1707): Gaceta de Madrid. The Catalan institutions abolished: Generalitat, Consell de Cent, local law codes. Elliott (2018), Scots and Catalans: places the suppression in European comparative context. The 1716 decree is explicitly referenced in current Catalan independence debates.

entries.label: Bourbon colonial reforms — the intendant system
entries.hint: Charles III replaces the old Habsburg colonial councils with a system of intendants (royal governors with wide powers) across Spanish America — the most systematic reorganisation of the colonial administration since the 16th century; it increases revenue but creates resentment.
entries.note: Ordinance of Intendants for New Spain 1786: AGI, Mexico. The colonial fiscal data showing revenue increase: TePaske (2010). Lynch (1958), Spanish Colonial Administration 1782–1810: the standard study. The resentment the reforms created: documented in subsequent independence movements' grievance lists (e.g. Miranda's writings). The reform's unintended consequence — creating a creole administrative class that then leads independence — is a major historiographical theme.

entries.label: Expulsion of the Jesuits — 1767
entries.hint: Charles III expels the Society of Jesus from Spain and its colonies with 24 hours' notice — 2,641 Spanish Jesuits deported; the reason is never officially stated; the expulsion closes Jesuit schools and missions across the empire.
entries.note: Pragmática Sanción 27 Feb 1767: Gaceta de Madrid 2 Mar 1767 — text published. The sealed orders to colonial governors: AGI and AGS. The number deported: 2,641 from Spain, more from the colonies — documented in Jesuit records. Batllori (1966), La Cultura Hispano-Italiana de los Jesuitas Expulsos: documents the exiles' intellectual activity. The expulsion's connection to the Enlightenment critique of corporate power: Herr (1958).

categories.label: Belief
categories.subtitle: National Catholicism under pressure — Enlightenment vs the Church

entries.label: The Inquisition under Bourbon reform — declining but present
entries.hint: The Bourbon Inquisition is significantly less active than its Habsburg predecessor — the last person burned alive at the stake by the Spanish Inquisition is María de los Dolores López (Seville, 1781); the 1826 execution of Cayetano Ripoll (hanging, by a diocesan board, not the Inquisition itself) is outside this folio's 1808 boundary; Goya witnesses the Inquisition's proceedings and documents them.
entries.note: AHN Inquisición: the archive continues into the Bourbon period. The Índice de libros prohibidos: published editions 1707, 1747, 1790 — BNE. The last actual burning at the stake by the Inquisition: María de los Dolores López, Seville, 1781 — convicted of heretical quietism. The 1826 Cayetano Ripoll execution (hanging, by a diocesan 'Junta de Fe', not the official Inquisition) falls outside this folio's 1808 boundary. Goya's Inquisition scenes: Prado (painted c.1812–1819). Kamen (1997), The Spanish Inquisition: covers the Bourbon period.

categories.label: Expression
categories.subtitle: Goya, neoclassicism and the Enlightenment aesthetic

entries.label: Goya — from court painter to witness of disaster
entries.hint: Goya is appointed court painter in 1786 and paints the royal family with merciless psychological honesty — his portraits of Charles IV's family (1800–1801) are the most penetrating royal portraits of the 18th century; he simultaneously documents the darkness beneath the Enlightenment surface.
entries.note: Goya's court appointment 25 Jun 1786: Archivo del Palacio Real. The Family of Charles IV (1800–1801): Prado (P000726). Los Caprichos (1799): published by Goya himself, sold in his shop — BNE holds copies. The Disasters of War etchings (made c.1810–1820): Prado. Goya's complaint to the Inquisition: AHN Inquisición (he was investigated for Los Caprichos). Hughes (2003), Goya: the standard modern biography.

entries.label: Ventura Rodríguez and Bourbon neoclassicism
entries.hint: Ventura Rodríguez is the leading architect of Spanish neoclassicism — his work for Charles III transforms Madrid; the Pilar basilica in Zaragoza (completed 1765) and the Pamplona Cathedral façade (1783) are the primary surviving monuments.
entries.note: Ventura Rodríguez's building records: Archivo de la Villa de Madrid. The Pilar basilica, Zaragoza: extant. The Pamplona Cathedral façade: extant. Reese (1976), The Architecture of Ventura Rodríguez: the standard study. Charles III's urban reform of Madrid (the Paseo del Prado planted 1775): documented in Archivo de la Villa de Madrid.

entries.label: Luigi Boccherini in Madrid — Spanish-Italian musical fusion
entries.hint: The Italian cellist-composer Luigi Boccherini spends 36 years in Madrid — his 'Spanish' style (incorporating fandango, seguidilla rhythms) creates a unique fusion; his chamber music is performed throughout Europe from Madrid manuscripts.
entries.note: Boccherini's appointment to Infante Luis's court 1770: Archivo del Palacio Real. His Madrid manuscripts: Biblioteca Nacional de España holds the primary collection. Gérard (1969), Catalogue of the Works of Luigi Boccherini: the standard catalogue. Boccherini's G major Guitar Quintet (Op. 30 No. 6) with fandango finale: the most widely performed example of the Spanish-Italian fusion.

categories.label: Thought
categories.subtitle: Spanish Enlightenment — Feijóo, Jovellanos and the reforming intellect

entries.label: Feijóo — Teatro Crítico Universal
entries.hint: Benito Feijóo's Teatro Crítico Universal (8 volumes, 1726–1739) attacks superstition and promotes scientific inquiry — it is the most widely read work of the Spanish Enlightenment, going through 14 editions in the 18th century; the Royal Council of Castile prohibits criticism of it.
entries.note: First volume 1726 (Madrid): BNE. The Royal Council prohibition on criticism (1750): Gaceta de Madrid. The 14 editions by 1800: documented in publication records. Herr (1958), The Eighteenth-Century Revolution in Spain, ch.2. Feijóo's influence on the subsequent generation of reformers: documented in their own writings.

entries.label: Jovellanos — Informe sobre la Ley Agraria
entries.hint: Gaspar Melchor de Jovellanos's Report on the Agrarian Law is the most important Enlightenment economic document in Spanish history — arguing for the abolition of entail, common lands and guild restrictions; it influences Spanish liberalism for a century.
entries.note: First published 1795 (Madrid): BNE. The manuscript submitted to the Council of Castile 1794: AHN Consejos. Jovellanos's subsequent imprisonment by Godoy (1801–1808): documented in AGS. Caso González (1998), Jovellanos: the standard biography. The Informe's influence on the Cádiz Constitution debates (1810–1812): explicit references in the Diario de Sesiones.

entries.label: Archivo General de Indias — the empire's colonial memory
entries.hint: Charles III orders all colonial documents consolidated in the Casa Lonja in Seville — 43,000 bundles covering the administration of the Americas and Philippines from 1492 to independence; the most complete colonial administrative archive in the world.
entries.note: UNESCO Memory of the World (1997). Founded by royal decree 1785: AGI itself. Holdings: 43,000 legajos, 80 million pages, 8,000 maps. PARES digital access. The archive contains: Cortés's letters, Pizarro's orders, the Laws of Burgos, Las Casas's petitions, every colonial governor's reports — the most complete record of a colonial empire's administration in existence.

categories.label: Subsistence
categories.subtitle: Colonial trade reform and the Bourbon economic programme

entries.label: Decreto de Libre Comercio — opening the colonial trade
entries.hint: Charles III ends the Cádiz monopoly on colonial trade — his Free Trade Decree allows 13 Spanish ports to trade directly with the Americas; colonial trade increases 700% between 1778 and 1796, the most dramatic economic expansion in Spanish colonial history.
entries.note: Decreto de Libre Comercio 12 Oct 1778: Gaceta de Madrid. AGI, Indiferente General: trade statistics before and after. The 700% growth figure: Fisher (1985), Commercial Relations between Spain and Spanish America in the Era of Free Trade. Lynch (1989), Bourbon Spain, ch.7. The decree's text is explicit about ending the Cádiz monopoly.

entries.label: Reales Fábricas — Bourbon state manufactures
entries.loc: La Granja (glass); El Buen Retiro (porcelain); Santa Bárbara (tapestries); Seville (tobacco)
entries.hint: The Bourbon royal manufactories are the centrepiece of mercantilist economic theory in 18th-century Spain — La Granja produces glass and crystal, El Buen Retiro produces porcelain to rival Sèvres and Meissen, Santa Bárbara produces tapestries from Goya's cartoons, the Seville tobacco factory (1728) becomes the largest industrial building in Spain.
entries.note: La Granja Real Fábrica de Cristales: royal foundation documents (AGS). El Buen Retiro porcelain manufactory (founded 1760): Archivo del Palacio Real. Santa Bárbara tapestry factory: Goya's cartoons held in the Prado — 63 cartoons documented. The Seville Fábrica de Tabacos (1728, completed 1757): extant (now University of Seville). Lynch (1989), Bourbon Spain ch.5: the standard survey.

categories.label: Exchange
categories.subtitle: Dynastic diplomacy, the Family Compact and the Atlantic world

entries.label: Family Compact — Bourbon alliance with France
entries.hint: Three Family Compacts between French and Spanish Bourbon branches (1733, 1743, 1761) coordinate policy against Britain — the Fourth Compact (Treaty of Aranjuez 1779) formally brings Spain into the American Revolutionary War; together they draw Spain into the Seven Years War, the American war and eventually Napoleon's orbit.
entries.note: First Family Compact 1733: AGS Estado; Archives des Affaires Étrangères, Paris. Third Family Compact 1761: AGS Estado. Spain's role in the American War of Independence 1779–1783: documented in AGS Marina and AGS Guerra. Dull (2005), The French Navy and the Seven Years' War: analyses the Family Compact's strategic consequences.

entries.label: Spanish Enlightenment and the Real Academia Española
entries.hint: Philip V founds the Real Academia Española in 1713 — modelled on the Académie française, its purpose is to 'purify' and standardise the Spanish language; its first dictionary (1726–1739) and grammar (1771) are the foundational documents of modern standard Spanish.
entries.note: Royal warrant founding the RAE: 3 Oct 1714, Gaceta de Madrid. Diccionario de Autoridades (1726–1739, 6 vols): BNE — the first comprehensive Spanish dictionary with literary citations. The 1771 Gramática: BNE. Lázaro Carreter (1949), Las ideas lingüísticas en España durante el siglo XVIII: the standard study. The RAE's founding documents: Archivo de la Real Academia Española, Madrid.

entries.label: Treaty of Aranjuez — the Fourth Family Compact
entries.hint: The Treaty of Aranjuez (1779) is the Fourth Family Compact — Spain joins France against Britain in the American Revolutionary War; Spain's war goals focus on recovering Gibraltar; the siege of Gibraltar (1779–1783) becomes the longest siege in British military history.
entries.note: Treaty of Aranjuez 12 Apr 1779: AGS Estado and Archives des Affaires Étrangères. The siege of Gibraltar 1779–1783: British National Archives (WO series) and AGS Marina. Dull (1985), A Diplomatic History of the American Revolution: analyses the treaty. Minorca recovered (1782): Treaty of Paris 1783. Gibraltar not recovered: the fundamental Spanish strategic failure documented.

entries.label: Treaty of San Ildefonso and the War of the Oranges
entries.hint: Godoy signs the Treaty of San Ildefonso (1796) — an offensive alliance with Revolutionary France leading directly to the War of the Oranges (Spain invades Portugal 1801 at Napoleon's behest) and to Trafalgar (1805); the treaty is the mechanism of Spain's fatal entanglement with Napoleon.
entries.note: Treaty of San Ildefonso 18 Aug 1796: AGS Estado. The War of the Oranges May–Jun 1801: AGS Guerra. La Parra López (1992), La alianza de Godoy con los revolucionarios: the standard study. The consequential chain: San Ildefonso (1796) → Trafalgar (1805) → Bayonne (1808) — documented in AGS and Archives des Affaires Étrangères.

categories.label: World
categories.subtitle: Bourbon Spain in its European and Atlantic context

entries.label: Peace of Utrecht — Spain's European losses
entries.hint: The Peace of Utrecht strips Spain of the Spanish Netherlands, Sardinia, Naples, Sicily and Gibraltar — the greatest territorial contraction in Spanish history; Spain retains the Americas but is reduced to a second-rank European power.
entries.note: Treaty of Utrecht: British National Archives (SP 108/402); Spanish ratification in AGS Estado. Gibraltar cession (Article 10): 'to be held and enjoyed absolutely with all manner of right forever without any exception or impediment whatsoever' — text still cited in contemporary diplomatic disputes. Kamen (1969), The War of Succession: the standard study of the peace terms.

entries.label: American independence movements — the empire's beginning of the end
entries.hint: The Bourbon reforms' success in enriching creole elites, combined with the example of the American and French revolutions, begins the process of independence — Tupac Amaru II's revolt in Peru (1780) is the largest colonial uprising before independence; Miranda's conspiracies begin in the 1790s.
entries.note: Tupac Amaru II revolt 1780–1781: documented in AGI Lima — the most detailed colonial uprising record. Francisco de Miranda's correspondence: Archivo del General Miranda (Caracas) and British Foreign Office records (Kew). Lynch (1973), The Spanish American Revolutions: the standard study. The American Declaration of Independence's Spanish translation: circulated in Latin America, documented in colonial records.

categories.label: Lives
categories.subtitle: Gender, sexuality & marginalised experience · evidence anchored

entries.label: Josefa Amar y Borbón — Spanish Enlightenment feminism
entries.hint: Josefa Amar y Borbón publishes the Discurso en defensa del talento de las mujeres (1786) and Discurso sobre la educación física y moral de las mujeres (1790) — the most substantial Spanish Enlightenment argument for women's equal education; she is admitted to the Aragonese Economic Society over male objections.
entries.note: Discurso en defensa del talento de las mujeres (1786): first published in Memorial Literario (Madrid) — BNE. Discurso sobre la educación (1790): BNE first edition. Her admission to the Aragonese Economic Society: documented in the society's minutes (Archivo de la RSEAP de Aragón). Bolufer Peruga (1998), Mujeres e Ilustración: the standard study of women in the Spanish Enlightenment.

entries.label: The Bourbon Inquisition and sodomy — declining but not abolished
entries.hint: Sodomy jurisdiction in Castile had always resided primarily with secular courts under the Catholic Monarchs' pragmática (1497); in the Crown of Aragon, the Inquisition held specific jurisdiction since a 1524 papal brief; Bourbon reformers curtail this Aragonese Inquisition jurisdiction across the 18th century as part of broader efforts to limit Church courts — but no criminal code formalising this was ever enacted; Spain's first Penal Code is 1822.
entries.note: The Catholic Monarchs' pragmática on sodomy (1497): documented — assigns jurisdiction to secular courts in Castile. The 1524 papal brief for the Aragonese Inquisition: documented in AHN Inquisición. The Bourbon restriction of Inquisition jurisdiction: documented in AHN Inquisición series — declining caseloads. Spain's first Penal Code: 1822 (Liberal Triennium). Lardizábal's unenacted reform draft c.1787: AHN Consejos — never approved. Berco (2007), Sexual Hierarchies: the standard study of sodomy prosecution across both periods.

events.l: Treaty of Aranjuez — Fourth Family Compact
San Ildefonso — Spain in Napoleon's orbit
